frameworks.name: low-dimensional shared coding
frameworks.one_line: Few orthogonal perceptual axes carry arousal across sensory modalities.

frameworks.name: cross-modal correspondence
frameworks.one_line: Stimulus features in one modality map systematically onto another.

core_question: Do emotional-arousal expressions across voice, face, script, and music share a common low-dimensional perceptual code?
key_assumptions: Arousal can be read out from abstract stimulus form, not only content
Perceptual dimensions are approximately orthogonal across modalities
manipulations: Cross-modal pairing of stimuli for matching judgments
Perceptual feature axes: spikiness, smoothness, periodicity
key_findings: Cross-modal arousal associations are stable across stimulus pairs and across subjects
Spikiness, smoothness, and periodicity account for most of the cross-modal correspondences
interpretations: A shared low-dimensional coding scheme for arousal survives modality crossings
Intuitive links between expressive form and emotional meaning are systematic, not idiosyncratic
